model.label: Claude Opus 4.6
model.description: Anthropic • Hybrid reasoning model that pushes the frontier for coding and AI agents, featuring a 1M context window.

pick.rationale: Arizona's historically elite defense—ranked #1 nationally with an AdjD of 89.7 in KenPom—is the decisive factor in this clash of titans, as it is uniquely equipped to slow Purdue's nation-leading offense (AdjO 131-132.6) in a way few other teams can. The roughly 7-point adjusted efficiency margin gap (+37.56 vs +30.5) is driven by Arizona's massive defensive superiority (AdjD 89.7 vs 100.5), which more than offsets Purdue's roughly 4-point offensive edge over Arizona's own 7th-ranked attack. While Purdue enters with tremendous momentum from their Big Ten Tournament championship run as a 7-seed, their mediocre 36th-ranked defense creates a critical vulnerability that Arizona's elite offense will exploit, and the Wildcats' 31-2 record, Big 12 Tournament title, and fully healthy roster with Koa Peat back make them the clear favorite to advance to the Final Four.

reasoningStep.title: West Regional Final: Arizona (1) vs Purdue (2)
reasoningStep.summary: Arizona's #1 defense should be the great equalizer against Purdue's #1 offense, while Arizona's own elite offense exploits Purdue's mediocre 36th-ranked defense to create a roughly 7-point adjusted efficiency margin advantage that favors the Wildcats.
evidence: Arizona KenPom #3: AdjEM +37.56, AdjO 127.2 (7th), AdjD 89.7 (#1 nationally), 31-2 record, Big 12 Tournament champions
Purdue KenPom #8: AdjEM +30.5, AdjO 131.0 (#2), AdjD 100.5 (36th), 26-8 record, Big Ten Tournament champions as 7-seed
Arizona's defensive advantage over Purdue's defense (~11 points per 100 possessions) vastly exceeds Purdue's offensive advantage over Arizona's offense (~4 points per 100 possessions)
Koa Peat returned healthy from February leg injury, Arizona at full strength
Purdue's 8 losses and mediocre defense suggest vulnerability against elite two-way teams like Arizona

result.text: Get To Know 2026 Big Ten Tournament Champs: Purdue Basketball Skip to content CBB Review (College Basketball Review) College Basketball Power Rankings, Interviews and more! ACC Boston College Cal Clemson Duke Florida State Georgia Tech Louisville Miami (Fla) NC State North Carolina Notre Dame Pitt SMU Stanford Syracuse Virginia Virginia Tech Wake Forest Big East Butler Creighton DePaul Georgetown Marquette Providence Seton Hall St. John’s UConn Villanova Xavier Big Ten Illinois Indiana Iowa Maryland Michigan Michigan State Minnesota Nebraska Northwestern Ohio State Oregon Penn State Purdue Rutgers UCLA USC Washington Wisconsin Big 12 Arizona Arizona State Baylor BYU Cincinnati Colorado Houston Iowa State Kansas Kansas State Oklahoma State TCU Texas Tech UCF Utah West Virginia SEC Alabama Arkansas Auburn Florida Georgia Kentucky LSU Mississippi State Missouri Oklahoma Ole Miss South Carolina Tennessee Texas Texas A&M Vanderbilt Mid Majors AAC Atlantic Sun Atlantic 10 C-USA Horizon MAAC MAC Missouri Valley Mountain West SoCon WAC WCC Oregon State Washington State Bracketology Matty Brackets March on CBB Review Rankings Meet Our Staff Get To Know 2026 Big Ten Tournament Champs: Purdue Basketball By Grace Jeffer Mar 18, 2026 Purdue basketball logo Purdue basketball became the first 7-seed in the Big Ten Tournament to win it all. With the season they’ve had, can they make a run in the NCAA Tournament as well? Purdue head coach Matt Painter has seen better seasons with the Boilermakers, but the team won four games over four straight days, exemplifying some great basketball. This will be Purdue’s 11th consecutive season earning a bid to the dance and its 9th consecutive year receiving a top-4 seed. How they got there: Purdue Basketball fans aren’t easy to please, so losing four of their last six games wasn’t exactly thrilling for their viewers. Expectations were high as Purdue opened as the No. 1 seed in the Associated Press Preseason Top 25 Poll. This was the first time in school history sitting at the very top during the preseason rankings. Before the Big Ten Tournament, Purdue was a projected No. 3 or No. 4 seed in the NCAA Tournament. The Boilermakers saw regular season losses to No. 10 Iowa State, UCLA, No. 11 Illinois, Indiana, No. 1 Michigan, No. 13 Michigan State, Ohio State, and Wisconsin. They did have some notable wins, however, against No. 8 Alabama, No. 15 Texas Tech, No. 21 Auburn, and Nebraska. Purdue’s Big Ten Tournament run included victories against Northwestern, No. 11 Nebraska, UCLA, and the 2025 reigning champs, No. 3 Michigan. Well deserved, Boilermakers 🚂🚂 #B1GMBBT x @BoilerBall pic.twitter.com/6kqRFdbsG6 — Big Ten Conference (@bigten) March 15, 2026 Taking home the Big Ten title certainly had an effect on their NCAA Tournament seeding, as many weren’t expecting them to be a 2-seed. result.text: Get To Know 2026 Big Ten Tournament Champs: Purdue Basketball Skip to content CBB Review (College Basketball Review) College Basketball Power Rankings, Interviews and more! ACC Boston College Cal Clemson Duke Florida State Georgia Tech Louisville Miami (Fla) NC State North Carolina Notre Dame Pitt SMU Stanford Syracuse Virginia Virginia Tech Wake Forest Big East Butler Creighton DePaul Georgetown Marquette Providence Seton Hall St. John’s UConn Villanova Xavier Big Ten Illinois Indiana Iowa Maryland Michigan Michigan State Minnesota Nebraska Northwestern Ohio State Oregon Penn State Purdue Rutgers UCLA USC Washington Wisconsin Big 12 Arizona Arizona State Baylor BYU Cincinnati Colorado Houston Iowa State Kansas Kansas State Oklahoma State TCU Texas Tech UCF Utah West Virginia SEC Alabama Arkansas Auburn Florida Georgia Kentucky LSU Mississippi State Missouri Oklahoma Ole Miss South Carolina Tennessee Texas Texas A&M Vanderbilt Mid Majors AAC Atlantic Sun Atlantic 10 C-USA Horizon MAAC MAC Missouri Valley Mountain West SoCon WAC WCC Oregon State Washington State Bracketology Matty Brackets March on CBB Review Rankings Meet Our Staff Get To Know 2026 Big Ten Tournament Champs: Purdue Basketball By Grace Jeffer Mar 18, 2026 Purdue basketball logo Purdue basketball became the first 7-seed in the Big Ten Tournament to win it all. With the season they’ve had, can they make a run in the NCAA Tournament as well? Purdue head coach Matt Painter has seen better seasons with the Boilermakers, but the team won four games over four straight days, exemplifying some great basketball. This will be Purdue’s 11th consecutive season earning a bid to the dance and its 9th consecutive year receiving a top-4 seed. How they got there: Purdue Basketball fans aren’t easy to please, so losing four of their last six games wasn’t exactly thrilling for their viewers. Expectations were high as Purdue opened as the No. 1 seed in the Associated Press Preseason Top 25 Poll. This was the first time in school history sitting at the very top during the preseason rankings. Before the Big Ten Tournament, Purdue was a projected No. 3 or No. 4 seed in the NCAA Tournament. The Boilermakers saw regular season losses to No. 10 Iowa State, UCLA, No. 11 Illinois, Indiana, No. 1 Michigan, No. 13 Michigan State, Ohio State, and Wisconsin. They did have some notable wins, however, against No. 8 Alabama, No. 15 Texas Tech, No. 21 Auburn, and Nebraska. Purdue’s Big Ten Tournament run included victories against Northwestern, No. 11 Nebraska, UCLA, and the 2025 reigning champs, No. 3 Michigan. Well deserved, Boilermakers 🚂🚂 #B1GMBBT x @BoilerBall pic.twitter.com/6kqRFdbsG6 — Big Ten Conference (@bigten) March 15, 2026 Taking home the Big Ten title certainly had an effect on their NCAA Tournament seeding, as many weren’t expecting them to be a 2-seed. modelTrace.content:
Now I have a comprehensive picture. Let me synthesize the data for this Elite 8 matchup:

**Arizona (1-seed)**
- KenPom #3: AdjEM +37.56, AdjO 127.2 (7th), AdjD 89.7 (#1)
- Torvik #3: AdjOE 126.2 (10th), AdjDE 91.1 (3rd)
- Record: 31-2, Big 12 Tournament champions
- Koa Peat returned healthy from leg injury in early March
- Elite two-way team with the nation's best defense

**Purdue (2-seed)**
- KenPom #8: AdjEM +30.5, AdjO 131.0 (2nd), AdjD 100.5
- Torvik #8: AdjOE 132.6 (1st), AdjDE 100.2 (36th)
- Record: 26-8, Big Ten Tournament champions (first 7-seed to win it)
- Nation's #1 offense, but mediocre defense (36th)
- Won 4 games in 4 days including beating #2 Michigan 80-72

The key matchup is Arizona's #1 defense vs. Purdue's #1 offense. Arizona has a 7-point adjusted efficiency margin advantage (+37.56 vs +30.5). Arizona's defense (AdjD 89.7) is roughly 11 points per 100 possessions better than Purdue's (100.5), while Purdue's offense (131.0) is only about 4 points better than Arizona's (127.2). The defensive gap is far larger than the offensive gap.

```json
{
  "pick": {
    "gameId": "e8-west-1",
    "winnerId": "arizona",
    "confidence": 0.78,
    "rationale": "Arizona's historically elite defense—ranked #1 nationally with an AdjD of 89.7 in KenPom and 3rd in Torvik (91.1)—is the decisive factor in this clash of titans, as it is uniquely equipped to slow Purdue's nation-leading offense (AdjO 131-132.6) in a way few other teams can. The roughly 7-point adjusted efficiency margin gap (+37.56 vs +30.5) is driven by Arizona's massive defensive superiority (AdjD 89.7 vs 100.5), which more than offsets Purdue's roughly 4-point offensive edge over Arizona's own 7th-ranked attack. While Purdue enters with tremendous momentum from their Big Ten Tournament championship run as a 7-seed—including an 80-72 upset of #2 Michigan—their mediocre 36th-ranked defense creates a critical vulnerability that Arizona's elite offense will exploit, and the Wildcats' 31-2 record, Big 12 Tournament title, and fully healthy roster with Koa Peat back make them the clear favorite to advance to the Final Four."
  },
  "reasoningStep": {
    "id": "e8-west-1",
    "title": "West Regional Final: Arizona (1) vs Purdue (2)",
    "summary": "Arizona's #1 defense should be the great equalizer against Purdue's #1 offense, while Arizona's own elite offense exploits Purdue's mediocre 36th-ranked defense to create a roughly 7-point adjusted efficiency margin advantage that favors the Wildcats.",
    "evidence": "Arizona: KenPom #3, AdjEM +37.56, AdjO 127.2 (7th), AdjD 89.7 (#1), 31-2 record, Big 12 Tournament champions, Koa Peat healthy. Purdue: KenPom #8, AdjEM +30.5, AdjO 131.0 (#2), AdjD 100.5 (36th), 26-8 record, Big Ten Tournament champions as 7-seed. Arizona's defensive advantage (11 points per 100 possessions) vastly exceeds Purdue's offensive advantage (4 points per 100 possessions). Purdue's 8 losses and mediocre defense suggest vulnerability against elite two-way teams."
  }
}
```

The roughly 7-point adjusted efficiency margin gap (+37.56 vs +30.5) is driven by Arizona's massive defensive superiority (AdjD 89.7 vs 100.5), which more than offsets Purdue's roughly 4-point offensive edge over Arizona's own 7th-ranked attack. While Purdue enters with tremendous momentum from their Big Ten Tournament championship run as a 7-seed, their mediocre 36th-ranked defense creates a critical vulnerability that Arizona's elite offense will exploit, and the Wildcats' 31-2 record, Big 12 Tournament title, and fully healthy roster with Koa Peat back make them the clear favorite to advance to the Final Four."},"reasoningStep":{"id":"e8-west-1","title":"West Regional Final: Arizona (1) vs Purdue (2)","summary":"Arizona's #1 defense should be the great equalizer against Purdue's #1 offense, while Arizona's own elite offense exploits Purdue's mediocre 36th-ranked defense to create a roughly 7-point adjusted efficiency margin advantage that favors the Wildcats.","evidence":["Arizona KenPom #3: AdjEM +37.56, AdjO 127.2 (7th), AdjD 89.7 (#1 nationally), 31-2 record, Big 12 Tournament champions","Purdue KenPom #8: AdjEM +30.5, AdjO 131.0 (#2), AdjD 100.5 (36th), 26-8 record, Big Ten Tournament champions as 7-seed","Arizona's defensive advantage over Purdue's defense (~11 points per 100 possessions) vastly exceeds Purdue's offensive advantage over Arizona's offense (~4 points per 100 possessions)","Koa Peat returned healthy from February leg injury, Arizona at full strength","Purdue's 8 losses and mediocre defense suggest vulnerability against elite two-way teams like Arizona"]}}
